metadata.title: Base security review rubric
metadata.description: Default severity rules for /gaia-security-review aligned with the OWASP Top 10. Layer 1 of the four-layer rubric composition; regimes (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS, SOC2) extend with additional categories on top of this base.

severity_rules.pattern: User-supplied data is concatenated into a SQL string instead of using parameterised queries or a query builder with bound parameters.
severity_rules.description: SQL injection allows attackers to execute arbitrary SQL — credential theft, data exfiltration, and remote code execution are common outcomes.
severity_rules.remediation: Use parameterised queries / prepared statements; never concatenate untrusted input into SQL.

severity_rules.pattern: User-supplied data is passed to a shell command via `exec`, `system`, `child_process.exec`, or backticks without strict allow-list validation.
severity_rules.description: Command injection gives the attacker arbitrary execution on the host. The blast radius is the entire process privilege.
severity_rules.remediation: Use the array-form `execFile`/`spawn` with a fixed binary; validate inputs against a strict allow-list.

severity_rules.pattern: User-supplied data is rendered into an HTML response without context-appropriate escaping (HTML-attribute, HTML-element, JS-context, URL-context).
severity_rules.description: Cross-site scripting (XSS) lets attackers run JavaScript in another user's session, stealing credentials or pivoting to admin actions.
severity_rules.remediation: Escape per output context; prefer a templating engine that escapes by default and use a strict CSP.

severity_rules.pattern: Password is stored using a fast or unsalted hash (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256 without per-user salt) instead of a memory-hard password hasher (bcrypt, scrypt, Argon2).
severity_rules.description: Weak password hashes are crackable at industrial scale; a database leak becomes a credential-stuffing event within hours.
severity_rules.remediation: Use bcrypt (cost ≥ 12), scrypt, or Argon2id with appropriate parameters; never roll your own.

severity_rules.pattern: Session token, JWT, or API key is generated from `Math.random()`, predictable inputs, or a sub-128-bit entropy source.
severity_rules.description: Predictable session identifiers let attackers forge or guess valid sessions, defeating authentication entirely.
severity_rules.remediation: Generate from a CSPRNG (`crypto.randomBytes(32)`, `secrets.token_urlsafe(32)`); never from the non-cryptographic RNG.

severity_rules.pattern: Authentication endpoint is not rate-limited and does not lock accounts after repeated failed attempts.
severity_rules.description: Unrestricted login endpoints enable credential stuffing and brute-force attacks at scale.
severity_rules.remediation: Add IP- and account-scoped rate limits; introduce progressive delays or temporary lockouts after failures.

severity_rules.pattern: Secret value (API key, private key, OAuth token, database password) is committed to the repository or hard-coded in the source.
severity_rules.description: Committed secrets must be considered compromised the moment the commit lands; rotation alone is insufficient if the repo is public or shared with vendors.
severity_rules.remediation: Move the secret to an environment variable or secret manager; rotate the leaked credential immediately.

severity_rules.pattern: Sensitive data (PII, PHI, payment data, auth tokens) is logged at info or debug level — visible in centralised log aggregators.
severity_rules.description: Logged sensitive data widens the trust boundary to every operator and downstream log processor.
severity_rules.remediation: Redact at the log call site; emit identifiers and metadata, never the sensitive payload itself.

severity_rules.pattern: Network call uses plain HTTP or accepts self-signed certificates without pinning — sensitive data crosses the network in clear-text or under a man-in-the-middle window.
severity_rules.description: Clear-text or unverified TLS exposes sensitive payloads to passive sniffing and active MITM attackers.
severity_rules.remediation: Require HTTPS/TLS 1.2+, validate certificates, and consider certificate pinning for high-value endpoints.

severity_rules.pattern: Authorization is checked only on the UI but not on the server endpoint — back-end handler trusts the client request without verifying the caller's role and ownership.
severity_rules.description: Client-side-only authorisation is bypassed by any direct API call. Insecure direct object references (IDOR) and privilege escalation follow.
severity_rules.remediation: Re-check role and ownership on every privileged server endpoint; add automated tests for unauthorised access.

severity_rules.pattern: Endpoint accepts a user-supplied object identifier and returns the object without verifying that the caller owns it (Insecure Direct Object Reference).
severity_rules.description: IDOR lets attackers iterate IDs to enumerate or mutate other users' resources.
severity_rules.remediation: Scope the lookup to the caller's tenant or owner ID; reject when the requested ID does not belong to the caller.

severity_rules.pattern: Service runs with default or example credentials in production, or admin endpoints are reachable without authentication.
severity_rules.description: Default credentials are the first thing an attacker tries; admin endpoints exposed without auth are catastrophic.
severity_rules.remediation: Force credential rotation at first boot; gate admin endpoints behind authenticated sessions and IP allow-lists.

severity_rules.pattern: Production response includes verbose error stack traces, framework banners, or detailed database errors visible to anonymous callers.
severity_rules.description: Verbose error responses leak technology stack and internal structure that lower the cost of follow-on attacks.
severity_rules.remediation: Return opaque error responses to clients; log the full stack server-side under a structured logger.

severity_rules.pattern: CORS configuration is permissive (`Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` combined with credentialed requests) or missing on a sensitive endpoint.
severity_rules.description: Permissive CORS plus credentialed requests defeats the same-origin policy and exposes user data to attacker-controlled origins.
severity_rules.remediation: Restrict allowed origins to a static allow-list; never combine `*` with `Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true`.
